output:
Using the SaaS Launch Audit skill to produce the full PASS/FAIL report.

---

# Launch Readiness Audit
**Product:** Developer CLI Tool | **ICP:** Full-stack engineers | **Launch:** Product Hunt, next week

---

## 1. Pre-Launch Validation

```
[FAIL] 50+ discovery calls completed
  → Next step: Document how many discovery conversations you've had with full-stack engineers — if under 50, conduct async surveys via X or Dev.to to close the gap before launch.

[FAIL] Pre-payment secured from 3+ customers
  → Next step: Reach out to your top 5 beta users this week and offer a lifetime deal or heavy annual discount in exchange for a credit card now — confirmed intent beats expressed interest.

[FAIL] Problem validated (not just interest)
  → Next step: Gather 3 specific quotes from users describing the pain in their own words, with measurable before/after outcomes (e.g., "saved 2 hours per deploy cycle") — this also becomes launch copy.

[FAIL] Manual service delivery tested
  → Next step: Onboard 3 engineers personally over a call, watch them run the CLI from scratch, and document every friction point — a CLI must be frictionless from `install` to first value.
```

---

## 2. Pricing Strategy

```
[FAIL] Pricing model is outcome/usage-based (not flat seat-only)
  → Next step: Your $19/$49/$99 flat tiers are per-seat subscriptions — consider adding a usage dimension (e.g., repos, pipelines, deploys/mo) so heavy users self-select into higher tiers. Flat pricing leaves revenue on the table for power users.

[FAIL] Value metric clearly identified
  → Next step: Define what your CLI delivers as an outcome (time saved, builds automated, errors caught) and make sure each tier boundary maps to that metric, not just "features."

[PASS] 3–4 tiers with clear differentiation
  (Developer / Team / Enterprise covers the right spread)

[FAIL] Annual contract incentives configured
  → Next step: Add a "2 months free" annual option (17% discount) to each tier — annual contracts improve cash flow and reduce churn before your retention data exists.

[FAIL] Pricing page live and scannable
  → Next step: Publish the pricing page before PH launch — visitors from Product Hunt will check pricing within 30 seconds and bounce if it's missing.
```

---

## 3. Payment Integration

```
[FAIL] Stripe account activated with live keys
  → Next step: Confirm live mode is enabled in Stripe dashboard and live publishable/secret keys are set in your production environment (not test keys).

[FAIL] Test transactions successful end-to-end
  → Next step: Run a full purchase flow using Stripe test cards for each of your 3 tiers, including upgrade, downgrade, and cancellation.

[FAIL] Webhook endpoints configured
  → Next step: Register your webhook endpoint in Stripe for at minimum: checkout.session.completed, customer.subscription.deleted, invoice.payment_failed — missing these will cause ghost subscriptions.

[FAIL] Billing portal accessible to customers
  → Next step: Enable Stripe Customer Portal and link it from your dashboard/app so users can self-manage billing without emailing you.

[FAIL] Tax/KYC requirements confirmed
  → Next step: Enable Stripe Tax or confirm your jurisdiction doesn't require VAT/sales tax collection for SaaS — EU and many US states require it at launch.
```

---

## 4. AI Economics

```
[N/A] All AI economics checks skipped — no AI/ML features in this product.
      (Gross margins should target 80–90% for a CLI tool with no inference costs — confirm your infra costs support this.)
```

---

## 5. GEO & AI Discovery

```
[FAIL] AI visibility audit completed
  → Next step: Search for your tool name and category (e.g., "best CLI tools for full-stack developers") in ChatGPT and Perplexity — if you don't appear, you need citation-worthy content published before launch.

[FAIL] Structured data / FAQ schema on website
  → Next step: Add FAQ schema markup (JSON-LD) to your pricing and homepage — this is the single highest-ROI action for appearing in AI-generated answers.

[FAIL] Content uses authoritative, direct language
  → Next step: Audit your homepage for hedging phrases ("might help", "could be useful") — replace with declarative outcomes ("cuts deploy time by 40%", "catches config errors before CI runs").

[FAIL] SSR / pre-rendering confirmed for LLM crawlers
  → Next step: Verify your site renders complete HTML on the server (not client-side only) — client-rendered SPAs are often invisible to AI crawlers.

[FAIL] Server response time < 200ms verified
  → Next step: Test your production URL with curl or Pingdom from multiple regions — fix any cold start issues before PH traffic hits.

[FAIL] Core Web Vitals passing
  → Next step: Run PageSpeed Insights on your homepage and fix any LCP > 2.5s or CLS > 0.1 before launch — this affects both SEO and perceived quality.
```

---

## 6. Competitor Intelligence

```
[FAIL] 3+ direct competitors identified
  → Next step: List at least 3 tools that full-stack engineers currently use for the same job-to-be-done — if you're not sure, ask your ICP "what do you use today instead of this?"

[FAIL] Competitor pricing documented
  → Next step: Screenshot and record the pricing pages of each competitor — use this to confirm your tiers are positioned correctly (you want to be cheaper than incumbents or clearly premium with justification).

[FAIL] Differentiation statement written (one sentence)
  → Next step: Write a single sentence: "Unlike [competitor], [your tool] [specific differentiator] for [specific outcome]" — this becomes your PH tagline.

[FAIL] AI visibility comparison done
  → Next step: Search "[your category] tools" in ChatGPT and Perplexity and note which competitors appear — if they're cited and you're not, read the GEO references guide before launch.

[FAIL] Citation sentiment analyzed
  → Next step: Review how AI tools describe your competitors — tone and framing reveal positioning gaps you can exploit in your launch copy.
```

---

## 7. Social Media & Launch Prep

```
[FAIL] Primary platform profiles created (GitHub, X, Dev.to, Reddit)
  → Next step: For a developer CLI tool your minimum viable presence is: GitHub org/repo (public, starred), X account, and at minimum one subreddit community engagement — create any missing profiles this week.

[FAIL] LinkedIn founder profile optimized for discovery
  → Next step: Update your headline to include the problem you solve (not your job title) and pin a post about the tool — LinkedIn drives B2B trial signups for developer tools.

[FAIL] X/Twitter presence established
  → Next step: Start posting build-in-public content now (1–2 posts/day) before launch so your profile has signal when PH visitors check it — an empty profile kills credibility.

[FAIL] Product Hunt profile ready to launch
  → Next step: Create your PH product page with final copy, screenshots/GIFs of the CLI in action, and a maker comment drafted — schedule it for 12:01am PT on launch day.

[FAIL] 400+ PH supporters lined up
  → Next step: This is your highest priority this week. DM your beta users, newsletter subscribers, Twitter followers, and anyone who expressed interest — ask them to upvote on launch day specifically (not before — only day-of upvotes count heavily).

[FAIL] Launch email sequence drafted
  → Next step: Write 3 emails: (1) "We're launching Tuesday!" announcement, (2) launch day "Go upvote us" with direct PH link, (3) post-launch "Thank you + here's what's next" — schedule all three now.

[FAIL] Content calendar for 30 days post-launch
  → Next step: Plan at least 3 posts/week for the 30 days after launch — changelogs, user wins, tips for full-stack engineers — this sustains the traffic spike into retained users.

[FAIL] "Magic Triangle" ready (founder content + outreach + retargeting)
  → Next step: Set up the triangle: (1) founder posts daily on X about the launch, (2) direct DM outreach to 50 targeted full-stack engineers on launch day, (3) a retargeting pixel on your site to recapture PH visitors who don't convert immediately.
```

---

## 8. Legal & Compliance

```
[FAIL] Privacy Policy published
  → Next step: Publish a Privacy Policy at /privacy before launch — use a generator like Termly or Iubenda, customize it for CLI usage/telemetry data if applicable, and link it from your footer.

[FAIL] Terms of Service published
  → Next step: Publish ToS at /terms covering acceptable use, no-refund policy (or your refund policy), and IP ownership — critical for enterprise tier customers.

[FAIL] Cookie consent banner configured
  → Next step: If you use analytics (GA4, PostHog, etc.) you need a cookie consent banner compliant with GDPR/CCPA — use Cookiebot or a lightweight alternative.

[FAIL] DPA template ready for Team/Enterprise customers
  → Next step: Prepare a standard Data Processing Agreement — enterprise buyers in EU will require this before signing, and your Team tier buyers at companies will ask for it too.
```

---

## 9. Trust Infrastructure

```
[FAIL] Auth system production-ready
  → Next step: Confirm your auth provider (Clerk, Auth.js, etc.) is on a paid plan with SLAs, and that session management, password reset, and MFA flows are all tested in production.

[FAIL] Audit logging enabled
  → Next step: Log auth events, plan changes, and key CLI actions per user — enterprise buyers specifically ask "can I see who ran what command?" and this is a deal-blocker without it.

[FAIL] Error tracking configured (Sentry or equivalent)
  → Next step: Install Sentry or Highlight.io on both your web app and CLI binary before launch — you will have bugs on launch day and need visibility into them in real time.

[FAIL] Uptime monitoring active
  → Next step: Set up Better Uptime, Checkly, or UptimeRobot on your API/dashboard before launch — you need to know before your users do when something goes down.

[FAIL] Security headers configured
  → Next step: Run securityheaders.com against your domain and implement at minimum: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, and HSTS — missing headers are a red flag for security-conscious developers evaluating your tool.
```

---

## 10. Launch Day Readiness

```
[FAIL] Support channels operational
  → Next step: Set up a public support channel before launch — at minimum a Discord server or GitHub Discussions. Developer tool buyers expect async community support, not email-only.

[FAIL] FAQ / documentation published
  → Next step: Publish docs covering: install, first command, auth setup, and common errors. Use Mintlify, Docusaurus, or even a GitHub Wiki — missing docs on launch day is the #1 reason developers churn immediately.

[FAIL] Analytics tracking verified
  → Next step: Confirm PostHog or GA4 is tracking signups, pricing page visits, and CLI installs before launch — without this you can't tell which PH traffic converted.

[FAIL] Team roles assigned for launch day
  → Next step: Assign explicit ownership: one person monitors PH comments and responds within 15 minutes, one monitors error alerts, one handles support DMs — covering all three simultaneously solo is a common failure mode.

[FAIL] Rollback plan documented
  → Next step: Write a one-page runbook: what breaks, who to call, how to revert the last deploy, how to communicate an outage to users — don't plan this under fire on launch day.
```

---

## Summary

| Section | Status |
|---|---|
| 1. Pre-Launch Validation | 0/4 PASS |
| 2. Pricing Strategy | 1/5 PASS |
| 3. Payment Integration | 0/5 PASS |
| 4. AI Economics | N/A |
| 5. GEO & AI Discovery | 0/6 PASS |
| 6. Competitor Intelligence | 0/5 PASS |
| 7. Social & Launch Prep | 0/8 PASS |
| 8. Legal & Compliance | 0/4 PASS |
| 9. Trust Infrastructure | 0/5 PASS |
| 10. Launch Day Readiness | 0/5 PASS |
| **Total** | **1/47 PASS** |

---

## Priority Order for Next 7 Days

**Do these first (launch blockers):**

1. **Stripe live keys + webhooks** — no payments = no business
2. **PH supporter outreach** — 400+ supporters take days to line up, start today
3. **Pricing page + docs live** — PH traffic will bounce without both
4. **Privacy Policy + ToS** — legal requirement, not optional
5. **Error tracking (Sentry)** — you will have launch day bugs

**Do these second (trust/conversion):**

6. Pre-payment from 3 beta users
7. Content calendar + launch email sequence
8. Discord or GitHub Discussions support channel
9. Uptime monitoring
10. Annual pricing option added

**Do before going to sleep the night before launch:**

11. Full purchase flow test on all 3 tiers (live Stripe)
12. PH product page scheduled for 12:01am PT
13. Team roles assigned in writing

assertions.text: The social or launch section recommends GitHub, Hacker News, and Reddit as primary platforms for a developer tool — not LinkedIn or TikTok as primary channels.
assertions.reasoning: The social section lists GitHub, X, Dev.to, and Reddit as primary platforms but never mentions Hacker News, which the assertion requires.

assertions.text: The Product Hunt preparation section specifies a concrete supporter threshold of 400+ supporters.
assertions.reasoning: The Product Hunt preparation section explicitly states '400+ PH supporters lined up' as a FAIL checklist item with that exact threshold.

assertions.text: The audit mentions the Magic Triangle concept — the combination of founder content, direct outreach, and retargeting.
assertions.reasoning: The audit explicitly includes a 'Magic Triangle' checklist item defined as 'founder content + outreach + retargeting'.

assertions.text: Each FAIL item in the report includes a single specific next step instruction.
assertions.reasoning: Every FAIL item in all visible sections includes a '→ Next step:' line with a specific, actionable instruction.

assertions.text: The audit covers all 10 sections: validation, pricing, payments, AI economics (marked N/A), GEO/AI discovery, competitors, social/launch, legal, trust infrastructure, and launch day.
assertions.reasoning: The output is truncated after section 7 (Social Media & Launch Prep) and is missing sections 8 (legal), 9 (trust infrastructure), and 10 (launch day).
